source.title: Rational computational design and development of an immunogenic multiepitope vaccine incorporating transmembrane proteins of Fusobacterium necrophorum

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Fusobacterium necrophorum is a Gram-negative anaerobe causing Lemierre's syndrome, bovine foot rot, and other necrotizing infections. Rising antimicrobial resistance and lack of effective vaccines motivate computational design of a multi-epitope vaccine targeting transmembrane proteins to elicit strong humoral and cellular immune responses.

section_gists.heading: Rational computational design and development of an immunogenic multiepitope vaccine incorporating transmembrane proteins of Fusobacterium necrophorum
section_gists.gist: Abstract/Introduction: F. necrophorum causes severe human and livestock diseases; no effective vaccine exists. Study aims to computationally design a multi-epitope vaccine from transmembrane proteins. Key results: vaccine construct with VaxiJen score 0.7293, solubility 8.30; 97.4% Ramachandran favored; >99% population coverage; strong docking with TLR7/8; MD simulations show stable RMSD ~4-5 Å, Rg ~36 Å; immune simulation predicts IgM, IgG, cytokine responses. Experimental validation needed.
process_signals: Problem: antimicrobial resistance and no effective vaccines
Goal: design multi-epitope vaccine targeting transmembrane proteins
Approach: computational pipeline from protein selection to immune simulation
Key outcomes: high antigenicity, stability, global coverage, strong TLR binding, predicted immune activation
Limitation: purely computational, requires experimental validation
methods: Protein selection based on antigenicity, non-allergenicity, non-toxicity, functional roles
Epitope prediction (B-cell, MHC-I, MHC-II) with defined thresholds
Vaccine construction with linkers and adjuvant
Structural prediction and validation (TrRosetta, Ramachandran, ERRAT)
MD simulations (RMSD, Rg, RMSF, PCA, energy)
failures_or_limits: Challenges in vaccine manufacturing and variable immune responses across populations mentioned

section_gists.heading: Methodology
section_gists.gist: Methodology section header only; no detailed content.

section_gists.heading: Protein selection and retrieval
section_gists.gist: Selected four transmembrane proteins (outer membrane efflux protein, SecA, FtsH, SecF) from UniProt based on high antigenicity (>0.4), non-allergenicity, non-toxicity, and roles in virulence (adhesion, invasion, biofilm). Subcellular localization confirmed by TMHMM. Antigenicity predicted by VaxiJen, allergenicity by AllerTOP, toxicity by Toxipred.
process_signals: Initial target selection: transmembrane proteins involved in virulence

section_gists.heading: Epitopes prediction
section_gists.gist: B-cell epitopes predicted by Bepipred 2.0 (HMM, surface accessibility) with score threshold 0.5. MHC-I epitopes by ANN 4.0 (IEDB) with IC50 <100 nM and percentile rank <1%. MHC-II epitopes by NN-align (IEDB) with IC50 <100 nM and low percentile rank.
process_signals: MHC-II: IC50 <100 nM, low percentile rank

section_gists.heading: Epitopes screening and evaluation
section_gists.gist: Final epitope selection based on antigenicity (VaxiJen), allergenicity (AllerTOP), and toxicity (Toxipred). No specific results or discarded epitopes mentioned.
process_signals: No mention of discarded epitopes or trade-offs

section_gists.heading: Population coverage analysis
section_gists.gist: IEDB population coverage tool used for MHC-I and MHC-II epitopes, focusing on globally prevalent alleles (HLA-A*02:01, HLA-B*07:02, HLA-DRB1*01:01). Coverage >99% reported in abstract.

section_gists.heading: Vaccine construction
section_gists.gist: Epitopes joined by linkers: GPGPG (between epitope types), KK (between B-cell epitopes), AAY (MHC-I), AYY (MHC-II). Beta-defensin adjuvant, Padre sequence, and 6xHis tag added at N-terminus. Flowchart shows overall methodology.
process_signals: Design choices: specific linkers for immunogenicity and processing

section_gists.heading: Physiochemical analysis
section_gists.gist: ExPASy ProtParam used for theoretical pI, instability index, amino acid composition, molecular weight, stability, aliphatic index, GRAVY. No specific results given.

section_gists.heading: Two-dimension structure analysis
section_gists.gist: PSIPRED and GOR IV used to predict secondary structure (coil, alpha-helix, beta-sheet). PSIPRED provides confidence scores.
process_signals: Secondary structure prediction for folding assessment

section_gists.heading: Three-dimension analysis
section_gists.gist: TrRosetta used for 3D structure prediction. Validation by Ramachandran plot (97.4% favored) and ERRAT.

section_gists.heading: Prediction of discontinuous B-Cell Epitopes
section_gists.gist: Ellipro used to predict discontinuous B-cell epitopes from 3D structure with default parameters (min score 0.5, PI 0.7).

section_gists.heading: Disulphide engineering
section_gists.gist: Disulfide by Design 2.0 used to increase stability. Criteria: Cα-Cα distance 4.4–6.8 Å, χ3 angle ±90° or ±120°, B-factor <30 Å². Residue pairs mutated to cysteine and energy minimized.
process_signals: Specific geometric and energetic criteria for disulfide bonds

section_gists.heading: Molecular docking and interaction analysis
section_gists.gist: ClusPro used for docking with TLR3,4,7,8. 30 runs per receptor, top model by lowest energy selected. TLR8(B) had highest interactions (13 H-bonds). MD simulations performed. Ligplot+ for interaction analysis.
process_signals: Hypothesis: vaccine binds TLRs to activate innate immunity
TLR selection rationale: recognition of bacterial components

section_gists.heading: Immune simulation
section_gists.gist: C-IMMSIM used to simulate immune response (humoral, cellular, innate). Assessed activation of T-cell, B-cell receptors. Abstract mentions increased IgM, IgG, cytokines.
process_signals: Predicted activation of both arms of immunity

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Transmembrane proteins of F. necrophorum contain epitopes that can be combined into a multi-epitope vaccine to elicit broad immune protection.
Specific linkers and adjuvant enhance immunogenicity and proper epitope processing.
The designed vaccine will stably bind to TLR7/8, triggering innate and adaptive immunity.
The vaccine construct is structurally stable and soluble, suitable for expression in E. coli.
Global population coverage can be achieved by selecting epitopes binding to prevalent HLA alleles.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Which specific epitopes were selected and how many?
Were any epitopes discarded during screening?
What were the exact physicochemical properties (pI, instability index, etc.)?
What were the secondary structure percentages?
What were the ERRAT and Ramachandran outlier details?
Which disulfide bonds were introduced and did they improve stability?
What were the docking scores/energies for each TLR?
What were the MD simulation details (RMSF peaks, SASA, PCA, FEL, MM/PBSA)?
What were the immune simulation quantitative results (antibody titers, cytokine levels)?
Were there any negative or partial results in any step?
How does the study address the lack of experimental validation?

events.summary: Rising antimicrobial resistance and lack of effective vaccines for F. necrophorum infections motivate alternative strategies.
Transmembrane proteins of F. necrophorum are promising vaccine targets due to immune accessibility and roles in virulence.
Selected four transmembrane proteins based on high antigenicity (>0.4), non-allergenicity, non-toxicity, and functional roles in virulence.
B-cell epitopes predicted with Bepipred 2.0 using score threshold 0.5; MHC-I epitopes with ANN 4.0 (IC50 <100 nM, percentile rank <1%); MHC-II with NN-align (IC50 <100 nM).
Vaccine construct built by linking epitopes with GPGPG, KK, AAY, AYY linkers; beta-defensin adjuvant, Padre sequence, and 6xHis tag added.
Structural validation with TrRosetta and Ramachandran plot confirmed 97.4% residues in favored regions, indicating high stability.
Population coverage analysis indicated over 99% global applicability.
Experimental validation is necessary to confirm safety and efficacy; challenges in manufacturing and variable immune responses must be addressed.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers abstract and introduction, outlining problem, hypothesis, design choices, validation results, and limitations.

events.summary: Disulfide engineering performed to increase vaccine stability using DbD2.0 with specific geometric and energetic criteria.
Molecular docking performed with ClusPro for TLR3,4,7,8; 30 runs per receptor, top model by lowest energy selected.
RMSD, RMSF, and Rg computed to analyze stability, flexibility, and compactness of vaccine-TLR8 complex.
PCA performed on MD trajectory to reduce dimensionality and evaluate conformational changes.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers disulfide engineering, docking, MD simulations, and various analyses (PCA, MM/PBSA, DCCM, FEL) for vaccine-TLR8 complex. Also includes protein selection results and B-cell epitope selection.

events.summary: MHC-I epitopes selected from four transmembrane proteins based on low percentile rank and IC50 < 100 nM.
MHC-II epitopes selected with antigenicity >0.4, non-allergenic, non-toxic, and IC50 < 100 nM.
Population coverage analysis of combined MHC-I and MHC-II epitopes yielded 99.39% global coverage.
Vaccine construct assembled by linking epitopes with specific linkers: KK for MHC-I, AAY for MHC-II, AYY for B-cell, and GPGPG for adjuvant.
Vaccine construct confirmed non-allergenic, antigenic (VaxiJen score 0.7293), and non-toxic.
Physicochemical analysis revealed pI 9.63, MW 40,307.71 Da, instability index 23.71 (stable), GRAVY -0.134 (hydrophilic).
Secondary structure prediction showed alpha helices 45.61%, beta strands 17.85%, coils 36.54%, indicating a predominantly helical structure.
Tertiary structure predicted by TrRosetta; best model had C-score 7.167, TM-score 0.52±0.25; Ramachandran plot showed 97.4% residues in favored regions.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed epitope selection, vaccine construction, and initial validation steps.

events.summary: Secondary structure analysis shows vaccine construct is predominantly helical with 45.61% alpha helices, 17.85% beta strands, and 36.54% coils.
TrRosetta predicted five models; the best model had C-score 7.167 and TM-score 0.52±0.25.
Ramachandran plot validation: 97.4% residues in most favored regions, 2.3% in additionally allowed, 0.3% in disallowed regions.
ERRAT quality factor for the improved 3D structure was 97.078.
Ellipro predicted eight discontinuous B-cell epitopes with scores 0.501–0.967; highest scoring epitope (0.967) is immunodominant.
Disulfide engineering introduced bonds at Ser133–Ser145 (energy 0.68 kcal/mol) and Gly193–Ile208 (energy 1.29 kcal/mol) to enhance stability.
MD simulation of vaccine-TLR8 complex: RMSD stabilized at 3–5 Å after 150 ns, indicating structural stability.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers secondary structure analysis, tertiary structure prediction and validation, discontinuous B-cell epitope prediction, disulfide engineering, molecular docking results, and MD simulation stability analysis.

events.summary: RMSD analysis of vaccine-TLR8 complex over 500 ns MD simulation indicates structural stability.
RMSF analysis reveals overall stability with some flexible regions, particularly near residues 350, 750, 800, and 850.
Radius of gyration analysis shows stable compactness of vaccine-TLR8 complex with minor fluctuations.
PCA scatter plot indicates structural similarity and conformational flexibility of vaccine-TLR8 complex.
Discontinuous B-cell epitopes predicted using Ellipro server, with scores and residues listed.
Disulfide engineering performed using Disulfide by Design 2.0 to enhance stability.
Molecular docking of vaccine with TLR3,4,7,8 performed, with energies and hydrogen bond interactions reported.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains MD simulation analyses (RMSD, RMSF, Rg, PCA, MM/PBSA), discontinuous B-cell epitope prediction, disulfide engineering, and docking results.

events.summary: DCCM analysis of vaccine-TLR8 complex reveals correlated and anti-correlated motions, indicating structural stability and functional flexibility.
Codon optimization for E. coli K12 expression reduced GC content from 61.85% to 41.72% and ensured high translational efficiency.
numbers_or_metrics: free energy of thermodynamic ensemble -438.35 kcal/mol

events.summary: In silico cloning of the 1059 bp vaccine construct into pET-29(+) vector using SnapGene, resulting in a 6430 bp recombinant vector.
Immune simulation predicts antigen production from day 3, antibody production from day 7 peaking at 5000 units by day 14, and increased IFN-γ and IL-20.
numbers_or_metrics: peaks at 3000 units, max 5000 units by day 14

events.summary: Computational vaccine design is a cost-effective and rapid approach for designing multiepitope vaccines.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed MD analysis (DCCM, FEL), codon optimization, in silico cloning, immune simulation results, and discussion of problem context.

events.summary: Immune simulation results show robust activation of humoral and cellular immunity with peak IgM and IgG response reaching 5000 units by day 14.
Vaccine construct has molecular weight ~61.48 kDa, pI 9.92 (basic), GRAVY 0.001 (balanced hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity), aliphatic index 82.45 (high thermostability).
Antigenicity score of vaccine construct is 0.7293 (VaxiJen), classifying it as highly immunogenic; population coverage 99.39%.
Comparison with Monkeypox vaccine: higher antigenicity score (0.7293 vs 0.45) and with Klebsiella pneumoniae vaccine: higher population coverage (99.39% vs 95%).
Molecular docking shows strong interactions with TLR7 (12 H-bonds) and TLR8 (13 H-bonds, -1434.6 kcal/mol binding energy).
Computational results require experimental validation; in vitro and in vivo studies needed to assess immunogenicity, safety, and protective efficacy.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains immune simulation results, physicochemical properties, docking outcomes, and comparisons with other vaccines, along with explicit limitations.
This chunk contains only reference list, acknowledgements, author contributions, declarations, and license information. No research evolution events are present.

evidence_bank.quote: The rise in antimicrobial resistance and the absence of effective vaccines underscore the need for alternative therapeutic strategies.
Transmembrane proteins present promising targets for vaccination due to their accessibility to the immune system and their involvement in bacterial adhesion, invasion, and virulence

These proteins were chosen based on their high antigenicity scores (>0.4), non-allergenicity and non-toxicity, along with their functional roles in bacterial virulence, including adhesion, invasion, and biofilm formation.
Only epitopes with IC50 values < 100 nM and percentile ranks < 1% were selected
The cut-off for selection was based on IC50 values < 100 nM and a low percentile rank
The linkers GPGPG, KK, AAY, and AYY were used to join the selected epitopes in the final vaccine construct
Structural validation using TrRosetta and Ramachandran plots confirmed 97.4% of residues in favored regions, indicating high stability.
Population coverage analysis indicated over 99% global applicability
experimental validation is necessary to confirm safety and efficacy, and challenges in vaccine manufacturing and variable immune responses across populations must also be addressed.
The selection criteria were the Cα-Cα distance (4.4–6.8 Å), χ3 dihedral angle (±90° or ±120°), and B-factor stability (<30 Å²).
Thirty docking runs were performed for each receptor (TLR-3, TLR-4, TLR-7, and TLR-8), and the top-ranked model with the lowest binding energy was selected for further analysis.
The complexes were analyzed using root mean square deviation (RMSD) to examine the stability, and the system also calculated root mean square fluctuation (RMSF) to assess flexibility.
The radius of gyration (Rg) was computed using GROMACS after converting the trajectory to XTC format with MDTraj.
The gmx covar command was used to compute and diagonalize the covariance matrix, generating the principal components based on the protein–protein trajectory.
two epitopes selected from each protein based on their low percentage rank with their corresponding IC50 values, specifically those less than 100

the epitopes with the lowest rank and an IC50 value of less than 100 were given priority
The analysis yielded a global value of 99.39% indicating the broad potential of the selected epitopes to elicit an immune response across diverse populations.
Eight MHC Class-I epitopes were linked using KK, eight MHC Class-II were connected via AAY and four B cell epitopes were joined using AYY linkers
a beta defensin protein sequence was added to the N-terminal N using a GPGPG linker
a 6X Histidine tag was incorporated at the C-terminus

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Fusobacterium necrophorum causes severe human and livestock diseases with rising antimicrobial resistance and no effective vaccines, motivating computational design of a multi-epitope vaccine targeting transmembrane proteins.

canonical_events.summary: Rising antimicrobial resistance and lack of effective vaccines for F. necrophorum infections motivate alternative strategies.
Transmembrane proteins of F. necrophorum are promising vaccine targets due to immune accessibility and roles in virulence.
Selected four transmembrane proteins (outer membrane efflux protein, SecA, FtsH, SecF) based on high antigenicity (>0.4), non-allergenicity, non-toxicity, and functional roles in virulence.
Predicted B-cell epitopes (Bepipred 2.0, score >0.5), MHC-I epitopes (ANN 4.0, IC50 <100 nM, percentile rank <1%), and MHC-II epitopes (NN-align, IC50 <100 nM).
Constructed vaccine by linking epitopes with GPGPG, KK, AAY, AYY linkers; added beta-defensin adjuvant, Padre sequence, and 6xHis tag.
Vaccine construct confirmed non-allergenic, antigenic (VaxiJen score 0.7293), and non-toxic.
Physicochemical analysis: pI 9.63, MW 40,307.71 Da, instability index 23.71 (stable), GRAVY -0.134 (hydrophilic).
Secondary structure: alpha helices 45.61%, beta strands 17.85%, coils 36.54%.
TrRosetta predicted 3D structure; best model C-score 7.167, TM-score 0.52±0.25; Ramachandran plot 97.4% favored, ERRAT 97.078.
Ellipro predicted eight discontinuous B-cell epitopes with scores 0.501–0.967; highest scoring epitope (0.967) is immunodominant.
Disulfide engineering introduced bonds at Ser133–Ser145 (energy 0.68 kcal/mol) and Gly193–Ile208 (energy 1.29 kcal/mol) to enhance stability.
Molecular docking with TLR3,4,7,8; TLR7 and TLR8 showed strong binding (TLR7: 12 H-bonds, TLR8: 13 H-bonds, -1434.6 kcal/mol).
MD simulation of vaccine-TLR8 complex: RMSD stabilized at 3–5 Å after 150 ns, Rg ~36 Å, stable energy -5000 kJ/mol.
Population coverage analysis: 99.39% global coverage using MHC-I and MHC-II epitopes.
Immune simulation (C-IMMSIM) predicts robust humoral and cellular response: IgM/IgG peak 5000 units by day 14, increased IFN-γ and IL-20.
Computational results require experimental validation; in vitro and in vivo studies needed to assess immunogenicity, safety, and protective efficacy.

edges.reason: Problem motivates hypothesis that transmembrane proteins are vaccine targets.
Hypothesis leads to selection of specific transmembrane proteins.
Selected proteins are used for epitope prediction.
Predicted epitopes are assembled into vaccine construct.
Construct is validated for antigenicity, allergenicity, toxicity.
Secondary structure analysis follows physicochemical analysis.
Docking identifies best TLR complex for MD simulation.
Epitopes used for population coverage analysis.
Positive simulation results lead to call for experimental validation.

must_cover_points: Problem of antimicrobial resistance and no effective vaccines
Hypothesis that transmembrane proteins are good targets
Vaccine construction with linkers and adjuvant
Molecular docking with TLR7/8 showing strong binding
